cards.text: A {{c1::constexpr}} class object can be evaluated at {{c2::compile time}}, allowing the object to be used in constant expressions.

To make a constructor constexpr:
{{c1::constexpr}} ClassName(int x) : m_x{x} { }

cards.front: What are the requirements for a constructor to be constexpr?
cards.back:
• Must be marked constexpr
  • All initializations must be constant expressions
  • Constructor body can only contain statements allowed in constexpr functions
  • All data members being initialized must be const or constexpr types

cards.question: What does this code demonstrate?
cards.code:
class Point
{
public:
    constexpr Point(int x, int y)
        : m_x{x}, m_y{y} { }
    constexpr int getX() const { return m_x; }
private:
    int m_x, m_y;
};

constexpr Point p{3, 4};
constexpr int x = p.getX();
cards.answer: Compile-time class object evaluation. Both the Point object and getX() call are evaluated at compile time.
cards.explanation: Constexpr constructor and member functions allow the entire object and its operations to be evaluated at compile time.

cards.text: Member functions called on constexpr objects must also be marked {{c1::constexpr}} (or {{c2::const}} in some contexts) to be usable in constant expressions.

cards.explanation:
Constexpr classes provide:
  • Compile-time computation and optimization
  • Ability to use objects in constant expressions (array sizes, template parameters)
  • Potentially better performance (computation done at compile time)
  • Stronger guarantees about object immutability
  • Can be used in contexts requiring compile-time constants
examples: int arr[Point{5, 10}.getX()];  // array size from constexpr

cards.front: What is an aggregate type in C++?
cards.back:
An aggregate is a class/struct with:
  • No user-declared constructors
  • No private or protected non-static data members
  • No base classes
  • No virtual functions
Can be initialized with brace initialization.

cards.question: Is this an aggregate?
cards.answer: Yes. It has no constructors, all members are public, no base classes, no virtual functions.
cards.explanation: Simple structs with only public data members are aggregates and can use aggregate initialization.

cards.text:
Aggregate initialization syntax:
{{c1::Data d{5, 10};}}  // members initialized in declaration order

cards.front: Can a constexpr object be modified at runtime?
cards.back: No. Constexpr objects are implicitly const and cannot be modified. They are initialized at compile time and remain constant throughout the program's execution.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.code:
class Counter
{
public:
    constexpr Counter(int n) : m_count{n} { }
    void increment() { ++m_count; }
private:
    int m_count;
};

constexpr Counter c{0};
c.increment(); // error!
cards.answer: Cannot call non-const member function on constexpr object. c is implicitly const and cannot be modified.
cards.explanation: Constexpr objects are const. You cannot call member functions that modify the object.
